Technical calculators for IT: latency, storage, SLA uptime, cloud costs. Decisions that scale.

## How It Works

Calculate hosts per subnet, subnet masks, and broadcast addresses instantly and for free.

This calculator applies the CIDR formula with your input values, verifying typical ranges and alerting you to values outside normal parameters.

## Important Notes

This calculation is for reference only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a specialized professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.a: CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation like /24 represents a network with a 24-bit network portion. The /n indicates how many bits define the network.

faq.q: How do you calculate usable hosts in a subnet?
faq.a: Use the formula: Hosts = 2^(32-n) - 2, where n is the CIDR number. You subtract 2 because the network and broadcast addresses cannot be assigned to devices.
/24 has 254 usable hosts, /25 has 126, and /26 has 62. Each additional bit halves the number of available addresses.
/30 provides 4 total addresses with 2 usable hosts—perfect for point-to-point router links and WAN connections.

faq.q: What is a subnet mask and why does it matter?
faq.a: A subnet mask (like 255.255.255.0 for /24) defines which portion of an IP address is the network vs. host. It's essential for routing and network design.

faq.q: What is VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)?
faq.a: VLSM allows different subnet sizes within the same network, enabling efficient use of address space without wasting IPs.
Results follow standard, internationally-validated formulas used in production networks. For critical decisions, verify with a networking professional.
Yes, all our calculators are free to use with no registration required.

faq.q: Do you collect or store my data?
faq.a: No. All calculations run in your browser. We never send your data to any server.
